title: iPad 10 vs. iPad Air Buyer's Guide: Is the $250 Difference Worth It?
content:
With the same design, display size, camera systems, and more, the entry-level iPad is now a formidable rival to the iPad Air at a markedly lower $349 price point. With $250 between these two ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ lines, how different are they and which should you buy?


Upon the discontinuation of the ninth-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ, Apple dropped the price of the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ from $449 to $349. This means that the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ is now $250 less than the $599 starting price of the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ that Apple just released.

The 10th-generation model completes the transformation of the ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ product lineup toward a flat look with squared-off edges, no home button, and an all-screen design with curved corners. With the exact same display size and identical features like a Touch ID top button, USB-C port, and 5G connectivity, many prospective customers may now be considering a purchase of the standard ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ instead of the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ ‚Äď but there are more differences between the devices than immediately meets the eye.

The M2 chip and double the amount of memory make the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ much more powerful than the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ. Combined with a more advanced display in two size options, support for Apple Pencil hover and the ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ Pro, a thinner and lighter design, and even a different selection of color options, many users still have good reasons to prefer the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ.

So should you consider buying the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ to save money, or do you need the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ instead? This breakdown serves as a clear way to see all the differences between the two devices.






















































































‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ (10th Generation, 2022) ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ (Sixth Generation, 2024)

sRGB color P3 wide color

Anti-reflective coating

Hardware-accelerated H.264 and HEVC
Video decode engine
Video encode engine

Wi‚ÄĎFi 6 connectivity Wi‚ÄĎFi 6E connectivity
‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ hover
Supports ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ (USB‚ÄĎC) and ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ (first generation) Supports ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ (USB‚ÄĎC) and ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ Pro

7mm depth 6.1mm depth

A key difference between the devices is their chips and amount of memory, so if you plan on using your ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ for more demanding tasks like 3D graphic design, advanced photo editing, and gaming, the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ will be the better choice by far. The ‚ÄĆM2‚ÄĆ chip's dedicated media engine is also be particularly helpful when video editing, and supports Stage Manager, Apple's multitasking system for the ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ.

The 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ's display lacks P3 wide color, full lamination, and an anti-reflective coating. While these aspects are unlikely to be major reasons to preference the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ, they are worth bearing in mind when trying to justify the $250 leap to the more expensive device. The ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ is also available in an all-new 13-inch size option, which is more suitable for multitasking and using the device as a laptop-replacement, but this pushes the price difference up to $450 ‚Äď more than the cost of the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ itself.

The ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ is marginally thinner and lighter, with differences that are so minor as to be unimportant to most customers, but the more muted tones of its color options may make it more or less appealing based on your personal preferences.

‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ support is also a key consideration. While the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ supports the first-generation ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ, the ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ with USB-C is a better choice due to easier charging. However, this lacks advanced features like pressure sensitivity, haptic feedback, and barrel roll that you get with the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ's ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ Pro support. People who plan to use the ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ heavily for tasks like note-taking and illustration will undoubtedly have a significantly better experience with the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ, which supports both the ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ with USB-C and the ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ Pro.

While both devices support external keyboards, they have different strengths. The 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ's Magic Keyboard Folio will be better for table-typers, those who prefer function keys, and those who want to easily detatch the keyboard but keep the ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ propped up on a surface, while the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ's Magic Keyboard is better for lap-typers and those who want backlighting.

In theory, the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ is a more compelling overall package with the ‚ÄĆM2‚ÄĆ chip, 4GB of additional memory, a dedicated media engine, ‚ÄĆStage Manager‚ÄĆ for multitasking, a better display, and a much better ‚ÄĆApple Pencil‚ÄĆ experience, but in practice, users with basic requirements are unlikely to notice a massive amount of difference between the devices. Unless you have specific need for the ‚ÄĆiPad Air‚ÄĆ's added features or its larger display size option, it may be worth saving the $250 and buying the 10th-generation ‚ÄĆiPad‚ÄĆ.
